text: The robotic liver segment seven resection begins with trocar placement in a straight line below the umbilicus, facing the upper right quadrant. The patient is positioned in the French position with left-sided decapitas. The surgical field is prepared with a 30-degree robotic optic, left bipolar grasper, right monopolar scissor, and double right tip-up grasper.

The surgeon begins by clearing the winged loviatus to facilitate the Pringles maneuver and prepare for liver-healer control. The right liver lobe is mobilized by dissecting the round ligament and falciform ligament, and clearing the main hepatic veins root using a robotic sealer. The right triangular ligament is sectioned to fully mobilize the right liver lobe. Monopolar scissors are used to remove tiny adhesions while preserving the diaphragm, avoiding pneumo-peritoneum. Tumor adhesions are carefully removed, and an aspirator tool is used as a retractor to evacuate smoke and blood during mobilization.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to mark the specimen limits before resection. The endocyanin green scope highlights the lesion in segment VII, followed by an intraoperative ultrasound revealing an undefined mass with unfactuous margins. A perimetral incision is made along the marked limits using monopolar scissors. The dissection proceeds in a crash-clamp manner, with bipolar forceps and an aspirator tool providing counter-traction. Small vessels within the parenchyma are individually cauterized with monopolar energy. The surgeon maintains a convex transection plane to control small vessels and ensure a clear surgical field. Larger third-order hepatic vein branches may require hemlock clamping before division.

The surgeon uses Maryland forceps for sharp dissection to control and clear vessels before placing hemlock clamps. The third order portal branch for segment VII (PE-VII) is carefully dissected and clamped. The right hepatic vein margin is maintained tight during the medial limit of the resection. The main segment seven branch from the hepatic vein is cleaned before being taken with a robotic wrist stapler.

The articulated jaws of the stapler are used to place sutures at the desired level, facilitating the removal of the specimen by transecting the remaining bronchial attachments and separating the segment from the liver. After completing the dissection, the surgeon releases the healer clamping and reviews hemostasis using bipolar forceps, occasionally applying hemostatic stitches. The under-wrist forceps perform delicate hemostatic sutures within the bronchyma, while bipolar forceps effectively control small bleeding. Hemostatic patches may be applied before concluding the liver resection.
